summary: This is an advice column where 'Aunt Pythia' (Cathy O'Neil) answers reader questions about academic research opportunities, breaking into data science, measuring societal fairness, and an off-color question about anatomy. The most substantive discussion centers on criminal justice inequality, where she argues that the real measure of unfairness should be the probability that making a mistake lands someone in prison—a probability that varies dramatically by race and class. She also provides practical advice for aspiring data scientists and reassures an undergrad that REU experience, while increasingly common, isn't strictly necessary for graduate school admission.
key_points: REU programs have proliferated since the 1970s and have become a common signaling device for grad school, but aren't strictly necessary—alternative research experiences like reading courses can substitute
For breaking into data science, start with Python, probability/statistics, then progress to machine learning, Bayesian statistics, and ethics
The prison system is a 'truly disgusting and predatory big business' that profits from incarcerating those too poor to be profitable otherwise
A better measure of societal fairness would be the probability that a mistake lands someone in jail—this varies dramatically by race due to discriminatory enforcement like Stop & Frisk
Everyone makes mistakes, especially young people, but the consequences of those mistakes are distributed profoundly unequally
money_quotes: If you're too poor for us to make money off of you directly, we will throw you in prison and make money off of your incarceration.
What is the relative chance that someone can get away with a mistake?

We all make mistakes, and young men (and women) are especially impulsive and judgment-lacking. So instead of asking whether they make mistakes, ask instead what the chances are that such mistakes will land them in jail or prison.

The rate of marijuana use is similar for whites and blacks but blacks are going to jail way more for possession, essentially because of Stop & Frisk. In other words, blacks are more likely to get caught.
I feel like those probabilities might be a good start at what you're getting at.
key_insight: True societal fairness should be measured not by whether people make mistakes, but by the wildly unequal probability that their mistakes result in incarceration.
